coredata.dc:title: Exceptional CO2 and H2S adsorption by tuning micro/mesopore ratios with embedded graphene oxide/N-doped carbon quantum dots in MIL-101(Cr): Experimental and computational insights

coredata.dc:description: Herein, a novel nanocomposite was developed to adjust the textural properties of metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) for adsorptive applications. To this end, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots/reduced graphene oxide nanocomposite (RC) was embedded into MIL-101(Cr) crystals, named RC-ML-x nanocomposites. The prepared nanoadsorbents were thoroughly characterized by different techniques. Results revealed that the main drawback of microporous MOFs, lack of mesopores, could be solved by embedding RC nanoparticles into MOFs, decreasing the micropore/mesopore volume ratio from 7.71 to 1.15. Optimizing the mesopore volume in RC-ML-1 dramatically improved the surface area and total pore volume by 40 % compared to pristine MIL-101(Cr). Adsorption experiments indicated that the sample containing 1 wt% had outstanding CO2 and H2S adsorption capacity of 25.79 and 34.15 mmol g−1 at 35 and 15 bar in 25 °C, respectively, elevated up to 15.80 % and 19.26 % compared to pristine MIL-101(Cr). This may be attributable to the cumulative effect of suitable micropore/mesopore volume ratio and the creation of the unsaturated metal sites and nitrogen functional groups by RC loading. In addition, the adsorption selectivity in different gas mixtures of CO2/CH4, H2S/CH4, CO2/N2, and H2S/N2 was analyzed by IAST. It was found that the samples containing 10 and 5 wt% had the highest selectivity toward CO2 and H2S, respectively, over N2 and CH4. Considering the simple approach adopted to tune the structure of microporous MOFs to achieve impressive gas adsorption and great cyclic capacity, the proposed RC-ML-x nanocomposites can be potential candidates for the adsorption and separation of CO2 and H2S.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, a novel nanocomposite was developed to adjust the textural properties of metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) for adsorptive applications. To this end, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots/reduced graphene oxide nanocomposite (RC) was embedded into MIL-101(Cr) crystals, named RC-ML-x nanocomposites. The prepared nanoadsorbents were thoroughly characterized by different techniques. Results revealed that the main drawback of microporous MOFs, lack of mesopores, could be solved by embedding RC nanoparticles into MOFs, decreasing the micropore/mesopore volume ratio from 7.71 to 1.15. Optimizing the mesopore volume in RC-ML-1 dramatically improved the surface area and total pore volume by 40 % compared to pristine MIL-101(Cr). Adsorption experiments indicated that the sample containing 1 wt% had outstanding CO2 and H2S adsorption capacity of 25.79 and 34.15 mmol g−1 at 35 and 15 bar in 25 °C, respectively, elevated up to 15.80 % and 19.26 % compared to pristine MIL-101(Cr). This may be attributable to the cumulative effect of suitable micropore/mesopore volume ratio and the creation of the unsaturated metal sites and nitrogen functional groups by RC loading. In addition, the adsorption selectivity in different gas mixtures of CO2/CH4, H2S/CH4, CO2/N2, and H2S/N2 was analyzed by IAST. It was found that the samples containing 10 and 5 wt% had the highest selectivity toward CO2 and H2S, respectively, over N2 and CH4. Considering the simple approach adopted to tune the structure of microporous MOFs to achieve impressive gas adsorption and great cyclic capacity, the proposed RC-ML-x nanocomposites can be potential candidates for the adsorption and separation of CO2 and H2S.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Adsorbents, as one of the most important components in removing polluting gases, can play a vital role in protecting the environment. In this regard, adjusting the properties of adsorbents is essential in applications demanding adsorbents with customized characteristics. To this end, two steps should be followed; first, the key parameters leading to the best adsorption of gas molecules should be recognized (including effective pore size); second, based on the synthesis processes, the characteristics of the newly synthesized adsorbents should be adjusted. Along with active site engineering, textural properties, including specific surface area, total pore volume, pore size distribution, micropore/mesopore volume ratio, etc., are effective characteristics of an adsorbent in gas phase applications. In most adsorptive applications, micropores can activate the pore-filling mechanism and improve the adsorption performance [1], and the presence of mesopores provides channels among micropores to proceed with the adsorption process [2].

Many materials have been proposed and studied due to the environmental importance of the adsorption process. Carbon-based materials [3–5], metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) [6–10], zeolites [11–13], metal oxides [14,15], and their composites [16] have been the most studied adsorbents. In addition, CO2 [17–19] and H2S [20–22] have been the topic of many research works in developing adsorbents. This is attributed to the severe effect of CO2 on global warming [23] and the poisonous nature of H2S [24], threatening the lives of humans and animals. The most effective pore size for CO2 capture has been shown to be within the range of 0.30–0.86 nm [25]. Additionally, detailed analyses have been conducted regarding the role of pore size in H2S adsorption, indicating that micropores are the most suitable sites for H2S uptake [26]. So, MOFs with significant micropore volume are one of the best candidates for H2S and CO2 removal, where the lack of mesopores can inhibit the adsorption process [27]. In many works, different strategies have been proposed to tune the micropore/mesopore volume ratio of MOFs. For instance, Alivand et al. have embedded carbon quantum dots into MIL-101(Cr) crystals to increase their mesopore volume for enhanced benzene and toluene adsorption [28]. Petit et al. [29] synthesized graphite oxide/HKUST-1 composite for H2S removal, where H2S molecules bonded to Cu centers and reacted with carboxylate groups of the MOF, leading to porosity loss. Liu et al. [30] prepared ZIF-8/Zn2GeO4 nanorods for CO2 adsorption in an aqueous medium, in which adding 25 wt% over Zn2GeO4 nanorods had 3.8 times higher dissolved CO2 uptake than Zn2GeO4 samples. Lin et al. [31] proposed a polyethyleneimine (PEI)/MIL-101(Cr) composite material which showed a CO2 adsorption capacity of 4.2 mmol g−1 at 0.15 bar and room temperature. In another work, MOF-505 containing graphite oxide had a CO2 adsorption capacity of 3.94 mmol g−1 at 25 °C and atmospheric pressure, attributed to micropores and open metal sites of the MOF [32]. In anotherstudy, Daraee et al. [33] successfully synthesized UiO-66/graphene oxide (GO) nanocomposites, exhibiting an impressive uptake capacity of 8.7 mmolS g−1 at 30 °C. This value is three times higher than that of the pristine UiO-66, due to the increased surface area and micropore volume resulting from the addition of graphene oxide. Other materials used to improve the adsorption properties of MOFs include ZnO [34], rice husk ash [35], mesoporous silica [36], and nanographite [37].

Separating CO2 from CH4 is another fascinating application of MOFs, which has been improved through embedding carbon-based materials into MOFs. Huang et al. [38] prepared a graphene oxide Cu-BTC composite for separating CO2/CH4. With a greater total pore volume and surface area than the pristine Cu-BTC, the aforementioned adsorbent exhibited a CO2 uptake of 8.19 mmol g−1 and a markedly improved CO2/CH4 selectivity. Alfe et al. [39] embedded graphene-like layers into BTC-based MOFs, where selectivity toward CO2 over CH4 was improved for Cu-based MOF. The selectivity and adsorption capacity of MIL-101(Cr) was improved by Zhou et al. [40], using graphene oxide, which supplied CO2 adsorption capacity of 22.4 mmol g−1 at 25 bar and 298 K, and good CO2/CH4 selectivity. In our previous work [41], have undertaken a novel methodology to enhance the selectivity of MOFs. Core-shell MOF nanocrystals, consisting of MIL-101(Cr) as the core and UiO-66(Zr) as the shell with a different pore size distribution, were designed and synthesized. These nanocrystals were engineered to possess distinct pore size distributions. The exceptional separation efficiency of H2S and CO2 molecules compared to CH4 and N2 was achieved as a result of the size exclusion separation facilitated by the precisely engineered pore diameters of MU nanocrystals.

Up to now, un-doped carbon-based materials have been used to adjust the surface properties of MOFs for the adsorption of gases. However, as well known, doping carbon-based materials can increase their binding energy toward adsorbates. Henceforth, for the first time in the current study, a novel nanocomposite was developed by embedding nitrogen-containing carbon-based composites into MIL-101(Cr) crystals. First, a composite of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) and reduced graphene oxide was synthesized. Then, the prepared nitrogen-containing carbon-based composite was embedded into MIL-101(Cr). After the thorough characterization of the as-synthesized nanocomposites, their capacity for adsorption of CO2, H2S, CH4, and N2 was studied and compared. We have successfully demonstrated the profound impact of a minute concentration of RC on the formation of structural defects, induction of mesoporosity, modulation of surface chemistry, enhancement of crystallinity, improvement of thermal stability, and optimization of adsorption and separation capabilities in nanocomposites. Subsequently, an investigation was conducted to evaluate the selectivity of the synthesized adsorbents in gas mixtures comprising CO2/CH4, H2S/CH4, CO2/N2, and H2S/N2. This assessment aimed to ascertain the potential of these adsorbents for utilization in separation processes. Finally, we investigated the isosteric heat and regeneration efficiency of the nanocomposites.

In this study, all reagents were of analytical quality and procured from Merck. These reagents include chromium nitrate nonahydrate (Cr(NO3)3·9H2O, 99 %), 1, 4-benzenedicarboxylic acid (H2BDC, 99 %), N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF, 99.8 %), nitric acid (HNO3, 65 %), ammonium fluoride (NH4F, 99.99 %), methanol (MeOH, 99.9 %), ethanol (EtOH, 99.9 %), citric acid monohydrate (C6H8O7·H2O, 99.5 %), urea (CH4N2O), hydrochloric acid (HCl, 37 %), potassium permanganate (KMnO4, 99.5 %), potassium nitrate (KNO3, 99 %), sulfuric acid (H2SO4, 98 %), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30 %), and graphite powder (GP). Deionized water, DI, was utilized as the solvent in all experimental procedures.

The synthesis of graphene oxide (GO) nanopowders was achieved using the modified Hummers method [42,43]. In summary, natural graphite powder (3 g) was oxidized by adding it to flask possessing 150 mL of H2SO4 (98 %) at room temperature. After cooling the flask to 0 °C and stirring it for 10 min, KNO3 (3 g) was added and the solution was stirred for 30 min to prevent agglomeration. In the next step, KMnO4 (18 g) was slowly added while stirring at temperatures less than 10 °C. After allowing the solution to cool to room temperature, it was subsequently heated to 35 °C and stirred for 2 h. Finally, 50 mL H2O2 (30 %), which was diluted in 200 mL DI water, was poured into the solution. In the last step, the obtained solution was centrifuged (9000 rpm), and the resulting powder was washed with 10 % (v/v) HCl solution and then with DI water to reach neutral pH, followed by drying at 60 °C for a duration of 24 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: According to our prior research, the hydrothermal approach was used to synthesize nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) [44]. In short, a clear solution was obtained by dissolving citric acid (4.2 g) and urea (3.6 g) in 100 mL DI water while stirring for 30 min. The resulting solution was kept in an SS autoclave and heated to 160 °C for 6 h. Following cooling to room temperature, centrifuging was done at 2000 rpm for 20 min. Finally, filtering of the N-CQDs was carried out, and they were dried for 24 h at 80 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of reduced GO (RGO) and RGO/N-CQDs nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: The hydrothermal method was employed to synthesize RGO/N-CQDs nanocomposites. In this method, after sonicating the GO solution (3 mg ml−1) for 1 h, the prepared N-CQDs (450 mg) were added to it with an equal mass ratio. Then, the solution was stirred for 30 min, and obtained suspension was added to the SS autoclave, and it was heated to 200 °C and kept for 10 h. Finally, filtration and washing with DI water were done to obtain the RGO/N-CQDs nanocomposite labeled as RC. In addition, to investigate the effect of nitrogen groups in the carbon-based materials on the adsorption capabilities of the modified MIL-101(Cr) adsorbents, another sample with the same procedure, without adding N-CQDs, was synthesized and labeled as RG.

Based on our previous work [41,45], MIL-101(Cr) was synthesized and then its nanocomposites with RC and RG were prepared. MIL-101(Cr), labeled as ML in figures, was synthesized by stirring Cr(NO3)3·9H2O (8 g) and H2BDC (3.32 g) in 100 mL DI water for 30 min. Then, after adding HNO3 (1.39 ml) to the prepared solution, it was stirred for an additional 30 min and transferred to the SS autoclave, which was subsequently heated to 220 °C and kept for 8 h. Finally, the powder was centrifuged out of the resulting suspension and washed with hot DI water to remove unreacted salt and added to a DMF solution at 80 °C and stirred for 8 h to eliminate the remaining H2BDC, which was unreacted during the synthesis process. After that, the powder was added to an aqueous NH4F solution (30 mM) and stirred for 5 h at 60 °C, followed by washing with boiling DI water (5 times), centrifuging, and soaking in the MeOH for three days to remove the solvents (i.e., DMF and H2O). Lastly, to polish the Cr sites of the MIL-101(Cr) crystals, the obtained powder was added to anhydrous EtOH at 80 °C and kept for 18 h. It was then activated at 150 °C for 24 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of RC-ML and RG-ML nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: In the final synthesis process, the prepared RC and RG were added to the MIL-101(Cr) to prepare the nanocomposites with modified surface properties and tuned micropore/mesopore volume ratios. In this regard, different amounts of RC, including 1, 5, and 10 wt% (compared to MIL-101(Cr)), were added to 100 mL DI water and ultrasound was applied to it for 1 h. In the next step, MIL-101(Cr) precursors were added to the solution containing RC, and the procedures described for MIL-101(Cr) synthesis were followed. The final product, labeled RC-ML-x (x = 1, 5, or 10 wt% RC), was collected. It is worth mentioning that to evaluate the effect of nitrogen groups of RC on the adsorption performance of MIL-101(Cr), another sample was prepared with 1 wt% RG without nitrogen groups, labeled as RG-ML-1. The methodology used for preparing RC-ML nanocomposites illustrated in scheme 1 .

The morphology and crystal size of the prepared adsorbents were studied using field emission-scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, TE-SCAN, Mira III) at 15 kV acceleration voltage and 50–200 kx magnification and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM, FEI Tecnai G20, USA) at 200 kV. Surface properties were obtained from nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms at 77 K using an ASAP 2020 analyzer (micrometrics, USA). The Brunauer, Emmett, and Teller (BET) analysis was used in order to compute the specific surface area. Additionally, the Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) equation was utilized in order to compute the pore size distributions and mean pore diameter by using adsorption isotherms. The t-plot approach was used to analyze the micropore volume as well as the external and micropore surface areas. Before conducting N2 adsorption–desorption measurements, the adsorbents underwent a 24 h outgassing process at 140 °C under vacuum pressure to eliminate undesired molecules from the micro-structures. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Thermo AVATAR, USA) was used to study the functional groups of the adsorbents. Crystallinity and phase purity were identified by the X-ray diffraction (XRD) method using a PW-1730 Philips instrument equipped with a Cu-Kα radiation source (λ = 1.5406 Å) and step size of 0.05. The current and accelerating voltage were 40 mA and 40 kV, respectively, and the scanning rate was 0.05 °s−1. The thermal stability of the adsorbents was addressed by performing the thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) in N2 atmosphere using a thermal analyzer (SDT Q600, TA, USA) from room temperature up to 800 °C with a heating ramp of 20 °C min−1. A TruSpec analyzer (LECO, USA) was used to quantify the C, H, N, and S contents. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed using a Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250 Multitechnique Surface Analyzer, equipped with an Al Kα X-ray monochromator and a pass energy of 20 eV.

Adsorption experiments were conducted based on the method explained in our previous work [44]. A schematic of the experimental setup to perform adsorption experiments is presented in Fig. S1 . The adsorption experiments were done on CO2, CH4, and N2 within the pressure range of 1–35 bar, and for H2S within the range of 1–15 bar and varied temperatures. The total uptake of the adsorbents was analyzed based on the volumetric method, as given in Eq. (1):

In this study, we examined RG-ML-1 and RC-ML-1 structures using the CP2K package [46], known for their effectiveness in adsorption analysis [47], using density functional theory (DFT). The Beck's parameter hybrid functional (B) and the Lee-Yang-Parr correlation functional (LYP), along with Grimme's DFT-D3 dispersion correction with zero damping, were applied to accurately account for weak dispersion forces [48]. The double zeta valence polarized (DZVP) basis set was boosted by Goedecker-Teter-Hutter (GTH) pseudopotentials to ensure precise modeling of complex molecular interactions [49,50]. We conducted the following molecular dynamics simulations by constructing a MOF of pristine MIL-101(Cr) with dimensions of 80 × 80 × 50 Å3. Additional layers of RG-ML and RC-ML were prepared to achieve a concentration of 1 wt%. We used the Forcite module within the Materials Studio software for all molecular dynamics (MD) simulations, which have previously shown good agreement with experimental adsorption results [51]. All mentioned nanosheets were relaxed through geometry optimization using a smart algorithm. Subsequently, we created a supercell to simulate RG-ML-1 and RC-ML-1 by coupling RG and RC onto the pristine MIL-101(Cr). The initial structures of RC, RG, and MIL-101(Cr), as illustrated in scheme 2 , provide a foundational overview of their configurations and relationships within the system. Surface/gas interface models were then established, incorporating a 20 Å vacuum layer to accommodate gas molecules. Finally, MD simulations were performed using a Universal force field to calculate adsorption energies. These simulations were conducted under the NPT ensemble at 15 bar, with a time step of 1 fs and a duration of 500 ps.

Characterizing the physical and chemical properties of nanoadsorbents can determine the most influential factors leading to the best adsorptive performance. First, to prove the embedding of N-CQDs in graphene sheets, elemental analysis (CHNS) characterization was done on the prepared RC and RG, showing 8.49 and 0 wt% nitrogen, respectively ( Table S1 ). Fig. S2 shows that the XRD patterns of the RC and RG nanostructures, which had two wide and low-intensity peaks at 2θ of 26.5° and 43°, respectively, corresponding to the (002) and (100) planes of the graphite structure, revealing the amorphously of the carbon structures [52]. In addition, the disappearance of the main peak of GO at 2θ = 10.5° confirmed its effective reduction to RGO through the hydrothermal method. The FT-IR results (Fig. 1
a) clearly show that in the RG structure, the peak intensity at 3421, 1737, 1384, and 1022 cm−1 significantly decreased, indicating stretching vibrations of OH, CO, C-OH, and CO, respectively. This may be the result of oxygen-containing functional groups reduction and RGO formation. In the RC composite structure, a strong and wide peak at around 3442 cm−1 shows the symmetric stretching vibration of NH. The characteristic peak at 1576 cm−1 indicates the presence of CC vibration in aromatic rings and is also assigned to pyrrole and CN [53]. Moreover, the strong peak at 1029 cm−1 is related to the CN vibration. Therefore, it confirms the existence of nitrogen-containing functional groups along with the increase in the hydroxyl group and carbonyl group contents in the presence of N-CQDs in the RC composite. XPS analysis was conducted to validate the coordination of N-CQDs on the RC surface, as evidenced by the high-resolution N1s spectra (Fig. 1 b). The spectrum was fitted with two Gaussian peaks at 399.98 and 402.38 eV, corresponding to pyrrolic-N and graphite-N, respectively. The predominant nitrogen species on the RC surface were pyrrolic-N species, which has been reported to enhance acid gas adsorption capacity. The FE-SEM and TEM images ( Fig. S3 ) revealed the insignificant difference between morphologies of RC and RG, both of which comprised thin carbon layers.

The XRD patterns of the pristine and modified MIL-101(Cr) samples are represented in Fig. 1 c. The XRD patterns of the pristine MIL-101(Cr) demonstrate characteristic peaks at 2θ ≈ 5.0°, 5.65°, 8.65°, 9.0°, and 16.45°, corresponding to the (311), (511), (822), (753), and (1395) planes, respectively. This is compatible with previous reports and the simulated XRD pattern of the MIL-101(Cr) [41,54]. In addition, the same trend was found for all samples in the way that all samples showed peaks at the same 2θ values. However, the intensity of peaks for the samples was different, indicating different crystallization degrees of the samples. This can be attributed to the role of RC nanoparticles as nucleation sites of the MIL-101(Cr) crystals [55], where different peak intensities and crystallization degrees were found based on the initial RC amount. For the sample possessing 1 wt% RC, the highest peak intensity and crystallization degree was found. This implies that 1 wt% is the optimum amount to reach the highest crystallization degree, further increase in the RC amount could not increase the peaks intensity compared to the RC-ML-1; however, other samples also had a higher crystallization degree than the pristine MIL-101(Cr). Moreover, no characteristic of the RC was observed, since the amount of RC nanoparticles was low, they were well dispersed within the MIL-101(Cr) crystal, or exfoliation of the RC and their small size.

The FT-IR spectra of the pristine and modified MIL-101(Cr) are given in Fig. 1 d. The same shape was observed for all samples, in which the absorption band at 587 cm−1 corresponds to the Cr-O bond, implying that the Cr-based MOFs were formed. Most of the peaks in the range of 750–1160 cm−1 are attributed to the CH bond of the H2BDC, and 1508 cm−1 is due to the CC stretching vibration [56,57]. Moreover, the peaks observed at 1402 and 1624 cm−1 are alluded to –COO- symmetric stretching, and the broad band at 3465 cm−1 is imputed to the stretching of hydroxyl groups (OH). As shown, the RC incorporation did not significantly affect the crystal structure and functional groups of MIL-101(Cr), which is consistent with the XRD patterns.

The thermal stability of the MIL-101(Cr) and RC-ML nanocomposites was investigated by the TGA analysis, as shown in Fig. 1 e. All materials showed the same behavior with increasing temperature, wherein the ranges of 25–110 °C and 110–350 °C, water and solvents were physically desorbed from the large and middle-size cages of the MOF structures. The weight loss after physical desorption (>350 °C) is attributed to the structure decomposition, which started with the H2BDC linker breakdown. At the final temperature of 800 °C, ML, RC-ML-1, RC-ML-5, and RC-ML-10 retained 27.31, 30.59, 34.82, and 33.72 wt% of their initial mass. However, the results revealed that the incorporation of a little RC did not disturb the thermal stability of the MIL-101(Cr) units.

To achieve a deeper understanding of the chemical composition and confirm the coordination of RC sheets within RC-ML crystals, XPS analysis was conducted. Although the high-resolution XPS spectra for Cr2p, C1s, and O1s showed striking similarities between pristine MIL-101(Cr) and RC-ML-1 ( Fig. S4 ), the high-resolution N1s spectrum for RC-ML-1 revealed a distinctive trace of RC (Fig. 1 f). Specifically, pristine MIL-101(Cr) displayed a single peak at 404.45 eV, associated with the nitro functional group. In contrast, RC-ML-1 exhibited an additional peak at 399.24 eV, which corresponds to pyrrolic-N within the RC structure. This finding strongly confirms the successful incorporation of RC nanoparticles into the RC-ML nanocomposites.

The adsorption–desorption isotherms of nitrogen, pore size distribution curves, and surface properties obtained from nitrogen physisorption are presented in Fig. 2 and Table 1 . The pristine MIL-101(Cr) demonstrated a type-I isotherm, which was completely reversible with sharp N2 adsorption at low partial pressures and without a hysteresis loop at high relative pressures, indicating its microporous structure. However, adding RC and RG materials to the pristine MIL-101(Cr) changed the isotherm type to type-IV, adjusted the porosity, and created a hysteresis loop, demonstrating the formation of mesopores. This can be clearly seen in the isotherms of RC-ML-5 and RC-ML-10, which possessed a higher RC amount. The evaluation of pore structure using the pore size distribution curves is illustrated in Figs. 2 b and S5, which was supported by the BJH calculation method. The figure reveals that the structures contained pores with 12 and 14–16 Å pore diameters, corresponding to pentagonal and pentagonal-hexagonal windows for MIL-101(Cr), RC-ML-1, RC-ML-5, and RG-ML-1, respectively.

As given in Table 1, the highest surface area was obtained for the samples containing 1 wt% RC and RG, as 3915 and 3383 m2 g−1, respectively, which was remarkably improved up to 40 % and 21 % compared to pristine MIL-101(Cr). The further increase in the RC amount could not increase the surface area to more than that of the 1 wt% RC. The improved porosity may be due to the separation of the MIL cages owing to the coordination of the nitrogen and oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of the graphene sheets with the metal sites of the MOFs. Moreover, the significance of adding carbon-based materials to the MIL-101(Cr) is to change their micropore/mesopore volume ratio. Although MOFs possess a high surface area and total pore volume, their low mesopore volume inhibits the adsorption capacity. In general, micropores can attract molecules through the pore-filling mechanism and mesopores provide channels to proceed with the adsorption and increase the capacity. The pristine MIL-101(Cr) had the highest micropore/mesopore ratio of 7.71, which decreased with the increasing amount of RC (Fig. 2 c). Since pristine MIL-101(Cr) and all modified MIL-101(Cr) sorbents had a relatively high surface area and total pore volume, their micropore/mesopore volume ratio can determine their final adsorptive performance.

The morphology and microstructure of pristine and modified MIL-101(Cr) were studied by FE-SEM and TEM analyses, as presented in Fig. 3 . For all samples, octahedral crystals were formed, similar to the reports in the literature [57,58]. According to Fig. 3 b (i-iv), the ML and RC-ML-1 crystals had a clear octahedral structure with a relatively narrow size distribution. However, with a further increase in the RC amount, the micrographs of RC-ML-5 and RC-ML-10 (Fig. 3 b (v-viii)) demonstrated an irregular octahedral morphology in which a significant part of the crystals were connected to each other and had obscure boundaries. It can be ascribed to the steric hindrance resulting from MIL-101(Cr) crystals growing over the RC sheets, which could induce a significant strain to cages of the MIL-101(Cr), stress the structure, resulting in smaller crystals with a deformed structure. Moreover, as seen in FE-SEM images, no RC nanoparticles were observed on the RC-ML composite surface. This can be attributed to the fact that RC nanoparticles also act as nucleation sites of MIL-101(Cr) crystals. Therefore, by increasing the RC amount, the crystal size of the MIL-101(Cr) was gradually decreased. As visually evident in Fig. 3 a-c, the pristine MIL-101(Cr) had the largest crystals, and the RC-MIL-10 possessed the smallest crystals. TEM images (Fig. 3 c) reconfirm the above-mentioned results, including the octahedral shape of crystals and the reduction of the crystal size. In particular, the embedded RC sheets within MIL-101 (Cr) crystals can be well identified in the HR-TEM images. Furthermore, the absence of needle-shaped crystals in the images confirms that the post-purification process was able to remove all unreacted H2BDC crystals. Additional FE-SEM and TEM images of the pristine and composite MOFs are given in Figs. S6 and S7 .

As illustrated in Fig. 3 d, comparing the elemental mapping of pristine MIL-101(Cr) and RC-ML-1 demonstrates a homogeneous atomic distribution, along with an enhanced N content on the RC-ML-1 crystal surfaces. Furthermore, supporting evidence from EDS graphs ( Fig. S8 ) verifies that C, O, N, and Cr are the only elemental components within the samples. Detailed elemental analysis of the MOFs is presented in Table S2 , indicating an increase in N content from 5.89 wt% in MIL-101(Cr) to 6.62 wt% and 7.40 wt% in RC-ML-1 and RC-ML-10, respectively. These findings align closely with the XPS analysis results, reinforcing the successful incorporation of RC nanoparticles into the composite framework.

CO2, H2S, CH4, and N2 adsorption isotherms of MIL-101(Cr) and RC-ML composites at various pressures and room temperature are shown in Fig. 4
. All isotherms were of Type I. Owing to the contact area of the gas molecules and MOFs, the highest adsorption was for H2S, followed by CO2, N2, and CH4. The strong electrostatic of H2S molecules and open metal sites and the surface of MOFs can be attributed to the polarity, high polarizability (38.0 × 10−25 cm3), and their ability to form hydrogen bonds. Despite the non-polarity of CO2 molecules, they present a relatively high polarizability, leading to significant adsorption, especially at low pressures. The non-polarity and low polarizability of N2 (17.6 × 10−25 cm3) and CH4 (26.0 × 10−25 cm3) molecules are responsible for their lowest adsorption capacities. This made the adsorption process hard, which could proceed through the pore-filling mechanism [44,59].

Notably, the highest adsorption capacity of all gases was found for RC-ML-1 due to favorable surface properties, including high specific surface area, high micropore and total pore volume, narrow pore size distribution, and more importantly, the desired micropore/mesopore ratio (6 > Ratio > 5). When the mesopore volume increases to improve the accessibility of micropores while keeping the pore width constant, the specific surface area and the adsorption capacity are enhanced (Table 1). However, the adsorption capacity of RC-ML-5 and RC-ML-10 were less than the pristine MIL-101(Cr). Considering the higher specific surface area of RC-ML-5 and RC-ML-10 compared to the pristine MIL-101(Cr), the lower adsorption capacity can be due to the non-uniform pore size distribution and lower micropore/mesopore volume ratios. The significant increase in the mesopore volume leads to the degradation of micropores and an increase in the mean pore diameter, which decreases the adsorption capacity. As seen in Table 2 , RC-ML-1 had an extraordinary adsorption capacity for H2S (34.15 mmol g−1 at 15 bar) and CO2 (25.79 mmol g−1 at 35 bar), which were enhanced by 19.26 % and 15.80 % compared to MIL-101(Cr), respectively. Comparing the results in Table 3 reveals that the RC-ML-1 nanocomposite had superior H2S and CO2 adsorption capability as well as textural properties compared to other MOFs previously reported.

The greatest difference in the adsorption capacity of the prepared sorbents was between RC-ML-1 and RC-ML-10 in the CH4 adsorption. RC-ML-1 and RG-ML-1, with micropore/mesopore volume ratios of 5.02 and 6.37, had the highest CH4 adsorption capacity. This indicates the significant role of the micropore/mesopore volume ratio in the CH4 adsorption performance, as the pore-filling mechanism serves as the dominant factor influencing the process. In addition, the presence of nitrogen-containing functional groups in the RC-ML-1 might also contribute to its higher adsorption capacity compared to RG-ML-1. This might be due to the presence of nitrogen-containing functional groups in the RC sample, which improved its electric conductivity. So, electron transfer within the adsorbents was facilitated, leading to the establishment of stronger interactions with gas molecules. Similar behaviors were observed for N2 adsorption on the synthesized MIL-101(Cr) and RC-ML nanosorbents. In the N2 adsorption experiment, pristine MIL-101(Cr) and RG-ML-1 had comparable behaviors, while RC-ML-1 showed a much higher adsorption capacity. Therefore, it can be inferred that the presence of nitrogen-containing functional groups in the RC-ML-1 had the highest contribution to the N2 adsorption capacity. The least significant difference was observed in CO2 adsorption, where RC-ML-1 and RG-ML-1, with a suitable micropore/mesopore volume ratio, had the highest adsorption capacity. The decrease in the difference can be due to the high polarizability of CO2 and interactions with nitrogen-containing functional groups over the surface of RC nanoparticles and more unsaturated metal sites, attributed to the increase in the dopant amount and structural defects in RC-ML-5 and RC-ML-10 compared to the pristine MIL-101(Cr). Consequently, it can be inferred that in addition to the surface properties, the increase in the amount of functional groups and active metal sites were determining factors of the CO2 adsorption capacity. Finally, the curves in Fig. 4 b show that RC-ML-10 had the lowest H2S adsorption capacity, confirming that the micropore/mesopore volume ratio should not be low. Moreover, the low difference between the H2S adsorption capacity of RC-ML-1 and RG-ML-1 also revealed the less considerable effect of nitrogen-containing functional groups compared to the favorable micropore/mesopore volume ratio. This reduced impact can be attributed to their low abundance, along with the ability of H2S molecules to interact with oxygen-containing functional groups and their capacity to form hydrogen bonds. From the results, it can be concluded that tuning the micropore/mesopore volume ratio by incorporating carbon-based materials can adjust the adsorption capacity of MIL-101(Cr)-based adsorbents significantly, while the presence of nitrogen-containing functional groups in the carbon-based materials can also improve the adsorption capacity through providing more active sites and better attracting the adsorbates.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity prediction by ideal adsorbed solution theory (IAST)
ce:section.ce:para:
Since, in many applications, the selectivity of an adsorbent plays an important role, here, the ideal adsorbed solution theory (IAST) was employed to study the selectivity of the prepared adsorbents in different gas mixtures. To this end, first, the experimental adsorption data were fitted on three well-known adsorption models, including Langmuir, Freundlich, and Sips, as given in Eqs. (2)–(4), respectively:

In these equations, qm denotes the maximum adsorption capacity per mass of an adsorbent at equilibrium (in mmol g−1), qe demonstrates the equilibrium adsorption capacity (in mmol g−1), b indicates the affinity coefficient (1/bar), n shows the deviation from the ideal homogenous surface, and P represents the equilibrium gas pressure (bar). After fitting the adsorption data and finding the best model in terms of statistical accuracy, the following IAST equation was used to determine the selectivity:
(5)

=

in which, the selectivity of gas 2 compared to 1 is given by S 2/1, y and x present the molar fractions in gas and adsorbed phases, respectively.

The fitting parameters and regression coefficients (R2) for H2S, CO2, N2, and CH4 gases are presented in Tables S3-6, respectively. For better understanding, the adsorption isotherms of each equilibrium model for MIL-101(Cr) and RC-ML-1, indicating the deviation of the model predicted equilibrium values from their corresponding experimental values, are illustrated in Figs. S9-
12. The Langmuir and Freundlich models successfully predicted all experimental data of equilibrium gas uptake on the as-prepared MOF samples. However, the Sips model provided the most accurate regression coefficients (R2 > 0.99) for all gas adsorption isotherms and was therefore selected to be used in IAST calculations. Selectivity was studied on gas mixtures of CO2/CH4 (10:90), H2S/CH4 (1:99), CO2/N2 (15:85), and H2S/N2 (5:95), according to industrial demands for treating sour natural gas or purifying N2 flows.

Fig. 5 demonstrates the IAST predicted selectivity of binary gas mixtures on the synthesized MOF samples as a function of operating pressure at 25 °C. As seen in Fig. 5 a, at low pressures (up to 10 bar), all adsorbents had close CO2/CH4 selectivities in the range of 12.93–16.46. However, by increasing the pressure, the difference in the selectivity was increased, and at 35 bar, totally different behaviors were observed. The RC-ML-1, which had the highest adsorption capacity of CO2 and H2S, showed the poorest selectivity at 35 bar, and RC-ML-10 provided the best selectivity of 60.19, which significantly improved the CO2/CH4 selectivity by 26.18 % compared to pristine MIL-101(Cr). RC-ML-10 showed the highest adsorption selectivity at all operating pressures. For CO2 removal from methane, a large increase in RC amount enhanced the selectivity, especially at high pressures. In desulfurizing CH4 (Fig. 5 b), a linear relationship between pressure and selectivity was observed for all adsorbents, and with increasing the pressure, H2S/CH4 selectivity was improved. RC-ML-1 showed the worst selectivity at all operating pressures, and the best selectivity was found for the RC-ML-5 (70.88 at 15 bar), followed by pristine MIL-101(Cr) (69.92 at 15 bar), RG-ML-1 (69.37 at 15 bar), and RC-ML-10 (62.32 at 15 bar). For purifying CO2 from N2, the same trend as CO2/CH4 was found, where by increasing the pressure, selectivity was dramatically improved. RC-ML-10 and RC-ML-5 presented almost the same behavior, with the final CO2/N2 selectivity at 35 bar being 24.01, elevated up to 20.23 % compared to pristine MIL-101(Cr). After that, the best selectivity was found for pristine MIL-101(Cr) (19.97 at 35 bar), RG-ML-1 (18.01 at 35 bar), and RC-ML-1 (16.48 at 35 bar). Finally, for H2S removal from N2, similar to the H2S/CH4 mixture, RC-ML-5 had the best performance with a significant selectivity of 111.28 at 15 bar.

Isosteric heat of adsorption, indicating the desorption heat for a constant adsorption capacity, was calculated using the data of the experimental adsorption isotherm at three different temperatures ( Figs. S13
and 14) and according to the Clausius-Clapeyron equation, as shown in the following:
(6)

∂ (

)
]
q e

in which, P shows pressure in bar, Qst indicates the adsorption heat in J mol−1, R denotes the ideal gas constant, and temperature is given by T in K. Based on this, it is possible to assess the thermodynamics of the adsorption sites.

The isosteric heat of adsorption for CO2 and H2S on pristine MIL-101(Cr) and RC-ML-1 are plotted in Fig. 6
a and b as a function of the gas adsorption, respectively. For CO2 uptake, both pristine MIL-101(Cr) and RC-ML-1 showed almost the same trend. This can demonstrate that by embedding the RC nanoparticles, increasing the number of active sites for CO2 adsorption had not been significantly effective, and only some channels were provided by mesopores to increase the adsorption capacity of the RC-ML-1. However, for H2S adsorption, RC-ML-1 showed higher Qst at all gas uptakes. This indicates that the RC-ML-1 had stronger electrostatic interaction and dispersive force toward H2S molecules. From this, it can be inferred that the creation of additional unsaturated metal sites caused by structural defects and the presence of nitrogen groups in the carbon-based materials could enhance the interactions with H2S molecules. In addition, the declining Qst values for CO2 and H2S gas adsorption on both MOFs were ascribed to the heterogeneity of adsorbents and reduced quantity of active sites on their surfaces.

In order for an adsorbent to be economically viable, it should be easily regenerated while retaining the adsorption capacity. Based on the adsorption results and the higher adsorption capacity of novel RC-ML-1 nanocomposites at all pressures for CO2 and H2S, it was considered for recyclability and regeneration efficiency studies. To this end, the adsorption capacity was carried out up to 35 and 15 bar for CO2 and H2S, respectively, and the regeneration performance after six consecutive adsorption–desorption cycles, its corresponding isotherms at 25 °C are presented in Fig. S15 , was obtained. To desorb the adsorbed gases and regenerate the adsorbent, after each adsorption cycle, the sample was heated at 140 °C and under vacuum pressure. The average regeneration efficiency was calculated by comparing the adsorption capacities of each cycle to the corresponding values of the fresh sample; subsequently, significant results were observed regarding the regeneration performance of RC-ML-1.

As shown in Fig. 7
a, in the second cycle, 98.27 and 97.48 % of the initial adsorption capacity could be retained for CO2 and H2S, respectively. This main drop in gas adsorption capacity is attributed to the partial saturation of open metal sites and nitrogen-containing functional groups during the initial gas adsorption. In the next cycles, the adsorption capacity was reduced negligibly and finally reached 95.21 and 93.99 % at the end of the 6th cycle for CO2 and H2S, respectively. Notably, in our previous work [45], we reported a lower regeneration efficiency for the pristine MIL-101(Cr) at the end of the 4th cycle. This indicates that the proposed adsorbent can be effectively employed in the long-term cyclic adsorption processes of CO2 and H2S up to high pressures and on industrial scales. The weaker cyclic performance of H2S after different 6 cycles can be attributed to the stronger interaction between H2S and the adsorbent, irreversible chemisorption with active sites, and its corrosive nature, which could almost partially damage the structure and block some pores.

The stability of the RC-ML-1 framework was assessed through XRD and TEM analyses following six H2S adsorption–desorption cycles. As depicted in Fig. 7 b, XRD patterns revealed consistent major peak positions between regenerated and fresh RC-ML-1 composites. Furthermore, low-magnification TEM images (Fig. S 16) clearly demonstrate that the polyhedral crystals of RC-ML-1 remained intact after six cycles. This evidence confirms that the H2S separation process does not destroy the primary crystalline structure or induce structural instability in RC-ML-1.

First, we investigated how nitrogen-doped atoms in RC affect the adsorption of gas molecules compared to RG alone. For the initial RG structure, we considered different functional OH groups and various positions of nitrogen doping in RC. Initial analysis using the van der Waals surface revealed that in RC, the surface density around O3 and O1 was higher than in other areas (Fig. 8
). Indeed, nitrogen doping altered the electronic properties of RC, increasing its ability to interact with gas molecules through changes in electron density. Functional OH groups enhanced polarity, facilitating hydrogen bonding or dipole interactions with gases. The higher surface density around O3 and O1 provided more reactive sites, making these areas more prone to adsorption. These factors collectively improved the material's adsorption affinity.

As initial systems, we considered various gas molecules, including CH4, H2S, CO2, and N2, between O3 and O1, Fig. 8. Table S8 presents adsorption energies of CH4, CO2, H2S, and N2 gas molecules on RG and RC. These energies, measured in kcal mol−1, reflect the strength of interaction between the gases and materials, with more negative values indicating stronger adsorption. Results show that nitrogen doping in RC significantly enhanced adsorption capabilities by altering the electronic structure of RG. CO2 showed the lowest substantial decrease in adsorption energy on RC (−57.57 kcal mol−1) due to its polar nature and interaction with nitrogen sites. H2S also experienced a notable decrease in energy, from −6.66 to –33.31 kcal mol−1, likely due to enhanced hydrogen bonding or dipole interactions. These results highlight that nitrogen doping significantly improved the adsorption efficiency of RG, especially for CO2 and H2S, making RC a promising material for gas capture and separation technologies.

To study the dynamic adsorption of gas molecules, we prepared the RC-ML-1 system by combining pristine MIL-101(Cr) with RC. For comparison, we also examined another pristine MIL-101(Cr) and RG-ML-1. The results are detailed in Table 4 . This approach helps us evaluate and compare the adsorption efficiency of different systems. The data shows that RC-ML-1 and RG-ML-1 outperformed pristine MIL-101(Cr) in adsorption capacity for all gases. RC-ML-1 had the highest capacities overall. Both systems were especially effective for H2S and CO2: RC-ML-1 adsorbed 19.78 mmol g−1 and RG-ML-1 adsorbed 17.30 mmol g−1 of H2S, compared to 14.98 mmol g−1 for MIL-101(Cr). For CO2, RC-ML-1 adsorbed 17.12 mmol g−1 and RG-ML-1 adsorbed 15.23 mmol g−1, surpassing MIL-101(Cr)'s 12.12 mmol g−1. These results highlight the enhanced adsorption capabilities of RC-ML-1, particularly for H2S and CO2, making it promising for gas adsorption applications. The results align with our experimental findings, confirming that RC-ML-1 exhibits superior adsorption capacities compared to RG-ML-1 and pristine MIL-101(Cr), particularly for H2S and CO2. This consistency underscores the enhanced effectiveness of the modified metal–organic frameworks in applications for gas adsorption and separation.

Herein, novel nanocomposite adsorbents (RC-ML-x), comprising nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots/ reduced graphene oxide (RC) and MIL-101(Cr) crystals, were prepared. All data obtained from XRD, TEM, and FE-SEM confirmed the formation of MIL-101(Cr) crystals with no trace of RC nanoparticles, indicating the coverage of RC nanoparticles by MIL-101(Cr) crystals. The micropore/mesopore volume ratio of the modified MOFs reduced from 7.71 for pristine MIL-101(Cr) to 1.15 for the RC-ML-10 sample, showing a reverse linear relationship with RC content. The MIL-101(Cr) sample modified by 1 wt% RC, with the optimal micropores/mesopores volume ratio of 5.02, showed an extraordinary surface area (3915 m2 g−1) and total pore volume (1.81 cm3 g−1), which increased by 40 % compared to pristine MIL-101(Cr). Consequently, the adsorption isotherms up to 35 bar indicated the highest adsorption capacity for it. Besides the interaction of additional open metal sites and nitrogen functional groups, the significant adsorption capacity of CO2 and H2S is attributed to the tuned micropore/mesopore volume ratio, high surface area, and total pore volume. We demonstrated that a certain increase in the mesopore volume, without increasing the average pore width, could provide access to more micropores and was one of the main reasons for the outstanding gas adsorption. Moreover, using IAST, the samples containing 10 and 5 wt% demonstrated the highest selectivity toward CO2 and H2S, respectively, over N2 and CH4. The isosteric heat measurement also showed that the sample containing 1 wt% RC had a higher adsorption affinity toward H2S than the pristine MIL-101(Cr). Cyclic adsorption experiments revealed the excellent reusability of RC-ML nanocomposites. Furthermore, DFT calculations confirmed that nitrogen doping in RC alters the material's electronic properties, creating highly reactive sites and boosting interaction potential, significantly improving gas adsorption and making it ideal for various industrial applications focusing on materials chemistry.
